skill.name: data-processing
skill.description: Process JSON, YAML, and TOML files with jq and yq command patterns for querying, filtering, transforming, and editing structured data.
skill.summary: Use jq and yq patterns to inspect, filter, transform, and update structured data files.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command, sensitive-key, network, and heuristic combination patterns. Manual review found the key/certificate, weak cryptography, and critical dangerous-combination alerts are false positives, with no prompt injection, malicious obfuscation, or credential exfiltration found. The skill is publishable with a warning because it uses Bash examples, includes one public curl example, and documents in-place file edits.

medium_findings.title: Bash-Based Local Data Processing
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for external command usage as a capability, not as malicious behavior. The skill teaches jq and yq Bash commands for local JSON, YAML, and TOML processing; these commands can read local project files and should be run only on intended inputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files directly contain Bash examples using jq, yq, loops, variables, and pipelines. The context is legitimate documentation, with no evidence of hidden execution or attacker-controlled command construction.

medium_findings.title: In-Place YAML Modification Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for filesystem modification risk. The yq examples include in-place edits that can change configuration files, so users should review targets before running generated commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented yq -i examples explicitly modify files in place. This is expected functionality, but the file-write behavior is clear and should be disclosed.

medium_findings.title: Public Network Example Uses curl
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for network usage, but no exfiltration was found. The only network example fetches a public GitHub API response and pipes it to jq for demonstration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The curl command and hardcoded GitHub URL are visible in the reference file. There is no evidence that local files, credentials, or environment values are sent over the network.

medium_findings.title: Config Queries May Surface Secret Values
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW as a data exposure concern. The skill includes examples for reading environment blocks and deeply nested fields, which may display sensitive values if used on secret-bearing configuration files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples query fields that can contain sensitive data, such as environment blocks or email fields. I did not find evidence of collection, persistence, or network transmission of those values.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Key Field Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for certificate or private key file access. The flagged text uses jq field names such as .key and keys, not certificate paths or private key material.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation examples for object keys and field access. They do not reference key files, PEM data, SSH keys, or certificate stores.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Command Substitution Is a Version Example
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for malicious shell substitution. The command substitution stores a package version from package.json in a local shell variable for later use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The substitution is static documentation and reads a local JSON field. It does not execute data from the file or pass untrusted values into another command in the shown context.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Match
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for weak cryptography. The cited skill description discusses data processing and does not name or use a cryptographic algorithm.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited line found descriptive marketplace text only. No MD5, SHA1, cipher choice, key generation, or cryptographic operation is present there.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Command Examples Using jq and yq
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on shell commands for its core workflow. This is appropriate for the skill, but commands should be reviewed before execution on sensitive repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The SKILL.md examples are explicit Bash command snippets. They are normal jq and yq usage, not obfuscated or automatically executed code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Pipeline to jq
dangerous_patterns.description: The advanced jq reference shows curl output piped into jq. The example contacts GitHub and does not include local data upload.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network command is present and uses a public API endpoint. No evidence found for credential exfiltration or covert endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: In-Place yq Edits
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference material includes yq -i examples that write changes directly to YAML files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The -i flag is clearly shown in multiple YAML modification examples. This is legitimate functionality with a clear file modification risk.

content.user_title: Process Structured Data from the Command Line
content.value_statement: Teams often need quick answers from JSON, YAML, and TOML files. This skill provides jq and yq patterns for querying, filtering, transforming, and editing structured data.
actual_capabilities: Extract fields, nested values, array items, and keys from JSON files with jq.
Query Docker Compose, Kubernetes, GitHub Actions, YAML, and TOML files with yq.
Filter arrays, transform objects, aggregate values, sort records, and format output.
Convert TOML or YAML content to JSON using documented yq patterns.
Show examples for in-place YAML edits, field deletion, field updates, and array additions.
limitations: Requires jq and yq command-line tools to be installed in the runtime environment.
Does not validate business meaning or schema correctness beyond requested queries.
Can expose sensitive values if used on secret-bearing configuration files.
In-place yq edits can overwrite files if commands are run without review.

use_cases.title: Inspect Application Configuration
use_cases.description: Extract package metadata, scripts, compiler settings, and lint rules from project configuration files.

use_cases.title: Review Deployment Manifests
use_cases.description: Find images, labels, services, volumes, jobs, and action usage across YAML deployment files.

use_cases.title: Prepare Structured Data Reports
use_cases.description: Filter, group, sort, and format JSON records for lightweight analysis and reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Extract a Field
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to show the jq or yq command that extracts the project name from my configuration file.

prompt_templates.title: Filter Records
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to build a jq query that lists active users and returns only their names and email addresses.

prompt_templates.title: Review Deployment YAML
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to inspect a Kubernetes deployment and list container images, labels, and resource names.

prompt_templates.title: Transform and Summarize Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create jq commands that group records by category, count each group, and sort the result.

output_examples.input: A developer asks for all npm script names in a package configuration file.
output_examples.output: The assistant provides a jq command that targets the scripts object and returns the script names as plain lines.

output_examples.input: A DevOps engineer wants every image referenced by a Docker Compose file.
output_examples.output: The assistant provides a yq command that reads the services section and prints each configured image.

output_examples.input: An analyst needs a count of records grouped by category.
output_examples.output: The assistant provides a jq pipeline that groups records, counts each group, and returns a compact summary.

best_practices: Run read-only queries first, then review any command that uses in-place editing.
Quote jq and yq expressions carefully so the shell does not alter them.
Redact secrets before sharing command output from configuration or workflow files.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated commands on unknown files without checking the target paths.
Do not use in-place edits before confirming the repository has a clean backup or version control state.
Do not pipe sensitive local data into network commands unless the destination is approved.

faq.question: What tools does this skill require?
faq.answer: It requires jq for JSON processing and yq for YAML or TOML processing.

faq.question: Can it edit files?
faq.answer: Yes. Some yq examples use in-place edits, so review commands before running them.

faq.question: Can it process Docker Compose files?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes yq patterns for services, images, volumes, and environment sections.

faq.question: Can it process Kubernetes manifests?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for resource names, labels, container images, and multi-document YAML.

faq.question: Does it send data to external services?
faq.answer: The skill is mainly local, but one reference example fetches public data from the GitHub API.

faq.question: Is it safe for files that contain secrets?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill can display values from configuration files, including environment blocks or other sensitive fields.
